---
title: "Introduction to ggplot2 Graphics in R"
format:
  revealjs:
    self-contained: true
    slide-number: true
editor: visual
---
<style>
.reveal {
  font-size: 160%;
}
</style>

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| include: false
library(ggplot2)
library(gridExtra)
library(knitr)
```

## ggplot2

- **Data** — default dataset to use for the plot
- **Mapping** — how variables are assigned to visual properties (`aes()`)
- **Layers** — geometric objects (points, lines, bars) that represent data
- **Scales** — maps data values to visual values (colors, axes)
- **Facets** — conditional plots for subsets of data
- **Themes** — appearance of all non-data elements

## Data

Every `ggplot2` plot starts with `ggplot()`. The first argument is the dataset.
ggplot2 works best with **tidy data** — rectangular data frames where rows are observations and columns are variables.

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| include: true
#| eval: false
ggplot(data = mpg)
```

## Mapping

Mappings connect dataset columns to visual properties using `aes()`.

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| include: true
#| eval: false
ggplot(mpg, aes(x = cty, y = hwy))
```

## Common aesthetics

- `x`, `y` — axis position
- `color` — outline or point color
- `fill` — fill color (bars, polygons)
- `shape` — point shape
- `size` — point or line size
- `alpha` — transparency (0 = invisible, 1 = opaque)

## Layers: geoms

Layers convert mapped data into visual elements. The most important component of a layer is the **geom** — the geometric object drawn.

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| include: true
#| eval: false
geom_point()      # scatter plot
geom_line()       # line plot
geom_bar()        # bar chart
geom_histogram()  # histogram
geom_boxplot()    # box plot
geom_density()    # density curve
geom_smooth()     # trend line
```

## The `mpg` dataset

Fuel economy data from 1999 to 2008 for 38 popular models of cars (EPA data, 234 rows × 11 variables):

| Variable | Description |
|---|---|
| `manufacturer`, `model` | car identity |
| `displ` | engine displacement (litres) |
| `year`, `cyl` | year of manufacture, number of cylinders |
| `trans` | transmission type |
| `drv` | drive train: `f` front, `r` rear, `4` 4WD |
| `cty`, `hwy` | city / highway miles per gallon |
| `fl` | fuel type |
| `class` | type of car |

## Example: Scatter Plot

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| include: true
ggplot(mpg, aes(x = cty, y = hwy)) +
  geom_point() +
  ggtitle("Scatter Plot: City vs Highway MPG") +
  xlab("City MPG") +
  ylab("Highway MPG")
```

> 📝 **Task 1** — change point color and size inside `geom_point()`

## Mapping color to a variable

Map a dataset column to the `color` aesthetic inside `aes()`:

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| include: true
ggplot(mpg, aes(x = cty, y = hwy, color = class)) +
  geom_point() +
  ggtitle("Scatter Plot: Color by Car Class") +
  xlab("City MPG") +
  ylab("Highway MPG")
```

> 📝 **Task 2** — add `size = displ` mapping, change color scale with `scale_color_brewer()`

## Fixed vs. mapped aesthetics

- **Inside `aes()`**: maps a data column → value depends on the data
- **Outside `aes()`**: fixed value → applies to all points equally

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| include: true
#| eval: false
# mapped — color varies by class
ggplot(mpg, aes(x = cty, y = hwy, color = class)) + geom_point()

# fixed — all points are darkgreen
ggplot(mpg, aes(x = cty, y = hwy)) + geom_point(color = "darkgreen")
```

## Adding multiple layers

Layers are added with `+`. Each layer shares the base `aes()` mapping unless overridden.

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| include: true
ggplot(mpg, aes(x = cty, y = hwy)) +
  geom_point(color = "blue") +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE, formula = y ~ x) +
  ggtitle("Scatter Plot with Linear Trend Line") +
  xlab("City MPG") +
  ylab("Highway MPG")
```

> 📝 **Task 3** — change `method = "lm"` to `"loess"`, try `se = FALSE`

## `geom_smooth` methods

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| include: true
#| eval: false
# Linear model (straight line)
geom_smooth(method = "lm",    formula = y ~ x)

# LOESS: locally estimated scatterplot smoothing (flexible curve)
geom_smooth(method = "loess", formula = y ~ x)

# Remove confidence interval ribbon
geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, formula = y ~ x)
```

## Scales

Scales control how data values are translated into visual properties — axis limits, breaks, labels, color palettes.

Use `scale_{aesthetic}_{type}()` functions:

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| include: true
#| eval: false
scale_color_viridis_d()                               # viridis palette, discrete
scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set1")                  # ColorBrewer, discrete
scale_x_log10()                                       # log10 on x-axis
scale_x_continuous(limits = c(10, 30), breaks = seq(10, 30, 5))
scale_y_continuous(limits = c(20, 45))
```

## Example: Viridis color scale

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| include: true
g1 <- ggplot(mpg, aes(cty, hwy, colour = class)) +
  geom_point() + ggtitle("Default color scale")

g2 <- ggplot(mpg, aes(cty, hwy, colour = class)) +
  geom_point() +
  scale_colour_viridis_d() + ggtitle("Viridis color scale")

grid.arrange(g1, g2, ncol = 2)
```

## Example: Log-scaled axis

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| include: true
g1 <- ggplot(mpg, aes(x = cty, y = hwy)) +
  geom_point() + ggtitle("Linear scale")

g2 <- ggplot(mpg, aes(x = cty, y = hwy)) +
  geom_point() +
  scale_x_log10() + ggtitle("Log10 x-axis")

grid.arrange(g1, g2, ncol = 2)
```

> 📝 **Task 4** — set custom axis limits with `scale_x_continuous()` / `scale_y_continuous()`

## Faceting: small multiples

Facets split data into panels based on one or more variables.

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| include: true
#| eval: false
facet_wrap(~ variable)             # wrap panels into rows/columns
facet_wrap(~ variable, ncol = 1)   # force single column
facet_grid(row_var ~ col_var)      # strict row × column grid
```

## The iris dataset

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="45%"}
![](iris_flower.jpg)
:::
::: {.column width="55%"}
Measurements in centimeters of sepal length and width and petal length and width for 50 flowers from each of 3 species:

- *Iris setosa*
- *Iris versicolor*
- *Iris virginica*

This famous Fisher/Anderson dataset is built into R — `data(iris)`.
:::
::::

## `facet_wrap` example

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| include: true
ggplot(iris, aes(x = Sepal.Length, y = Sepal.Width, color = Species)) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  facet_wrap(~ Species) +
  ggtitle("Faceted Scatter Plot: Iris Data") +
  xlab("Sepal Length") +
  ylab("Sepal Width")
```

> 📝 **Task 5** — try `ncol = 1` and `nrow = 1` in `facet_wrap()`

## `facet_grid` example

Rows and columns defined by two variables:

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| include: true
ggplot(mpg, aes(x = cty, y = hwy, color = class)) +
  geom_point() +
  facet_grid(drv ~ cyl) +
  ggtitle("MPG by Drive Type and Cylinder Count") +
  xlab("City MPG") +
  ylab("Highway MPG")
```

## Coordinates

`coord_flip()` swaps x and y axes:

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| include: true
ggplot(mtcars, aes(x = factor(cyl), y = mpg)) +
  geom_boxplot(fill = "lightblue", color = "darkblue") +
  coord_flip() +
  theme_minimal() +
  ggtitle("Box Plot: MPG by Cylinder Count (Flipped)")
```

## Built-in themes

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| include: true
#| eval: false
theme_gray()     # default — grey background, white gridlines
theme_minimal()  # minimal chrome, no background
theme_classic()  # no gridlines, classic look
theme_dark()     # dark background
theme_bw()       # black-and-white, suitable for print
```

## Customizing with `theme()`

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| include: true
ggplot(mpg, aes(x = cty, y = hwy, color = class)) +
  geom_point() +
  theme_classic() +
  theme(
    legend.position = "top",
    axis.line = element_line(size = 0.75, color = "gray"),
    text = element_text(size = 12)
  ) +
  ggtitle("Scatter Plot with Custom Theme")
```

> 📝 **Task 6** — switch to `theme_dark()`, adjust font size with `text = element_text(size = 14)`

## Bar plot

`geom_bar()` counts observations in each group automatically:

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| include: true
ggplot(mpg, aes(x = class)) +
  geom_bar(fill = "steelblue") +
  ggtitle("Bar Plot: Count of Car Classes") +
  xlab("Car Class") +
  ylab("Count")
```

## Bar plot: pre-computed counts

When you already have counts, use `stat = "identity"`:

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| include: true
x <- c(Mazda = 10, Toyota = 20, Honda = 30)
ggplot(data.frame(x = names(x), y = x), aes(x, y)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", fill = "steelblue") +
  xlab("Brand") + ylab("Count")
```

## Line plot

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| include: true
df_line <- data.frame(x = 1:10, y = (1:10)^2)
ggplot(df_line, aes(x = x, y = y)) +
  geom_line(color = "red", size = 1.2) +
  ggtitle("Line Plot: y = x²") +
  xlab("X") + ylab("Y")
```

> 📝 **Task** — add points to this line plot with `geom_point()`

## Line plot: multiple lines

Using the `Orange` dataset (tree circumference over time):

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| include: true
kable(head(Orange))
```

## Line plot: multiple lines

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| include: true
ggplot(Orange, aes(x = age, y = circumference, color = Tree)) +
  geom_line() +
  ggtitle("Orange Tree Growth") +
  xlab("Age (days)") + ylab("Circumference (mm)")
```

## Box plot

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| include: true
ggplot(mtcars, aes(x = factor(cyl), y = mpg)) +
  geom_boxplot(fill = "orange") +
  ggtitle("Box Plot: MPG by Cylinder Count") +
  xlab("Cylinders") +
  ylab("MPG")
```

## Histogram

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| include: true
ggplot(iris, aes(x = Sepal.Length)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 20, fill = "purple", alpha = 0.7) +
  ggtitle("Histogram of Sepal Length") +
  xlab("Sepal Length") + ylab("Frequency")
```

## Histogram: color by group

`position` controls how bars for different groups are drawn:

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| include: true
#| eval: false
position = "identity"  # overlap (use alpha < 1)
position = "stack"     # stack on top of each other
position = "dodge"     # side by side
position = "fill"      # stack, normalized to 1
```

## Histogram: overlapping by species

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| include: true
ggplot(iris, aes(x = Sepal.Length, fill = Species)) +
  geom_histogram(alpha = 0.5, bins = 20, position = "identity") +
  ggtitle("Sepal Length by Species") +
  xlab("Sepal Length") + ylab("Count")
```

> 📝 **Task** — try `position = "dodge"`, `"stack"`, `"fill"` and `facet_wrap(~ Species)`

## Histogram: faceted by species

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| include: true
ggplot(iris, aes(x = Sepal.Length, fill = Species)) +
  geom_histogram(alpha = 0.5, bins = 20) +
  facet_grid(Species ~ .) +
  ggtitle("Sepal Length — Faceted by Species")
```

## Density plot

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| include: true
ggplot(iris, aes(x = Sepal.Length, fill = Species)) +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.5) +
  ggtitle("Density Plot of Sepal Length by Species") +
  xlab("Sepal Length") + ylab("Density")
```

> 📝 **Task 7** — customize one plot type: colors, labels, alpha, theme, or new dataset

## `gridExtra::grid.arrange()`

Arrange multiple `ggplot` objects in one figure:

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| include: true
#| eval: false
library(gridExtra)
grid.arrange(p1, p2, p3, ncol = 2)
grid.arrange(p1, p2, p3, nrow = 1)
grid.arrange(p1, p2, p3,
             layout_matrix = rbind(c(1, 1),   # p1 spans top row
                                   c(2, 3)))  # p2 and p3 on bottom
```

## `grid.arrange` example

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| include: true
p_density <- ggplot(iris, aes(x = Sepal.Length, fill = Species)) +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.5) + facet_grid(Species ~ .)
p_line <- ggplot(Orange, aes(x = age, y = circumference, color = Tree)) +
  geom_line()
p_box  <- ggplot(mtcars, aes(x = factor(cyl), y = mpg)) +
  geom_boxplot()

grid.arrange(p_density, p_line, p_box, ncol = 2)
```

## Custom layout with `layout_matrix`

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| include: true
grid.arrange(p_density, p_line, p_box,
             layout_matrix = rbind(c(1, 1), c(2, 3)),
             heights = c(2, 1))
```

> 📝 **Task 8** — try different `nrow`/`ncol`, custom `layout_matrix`, add your own plots

## `ggsave()`

`ggsave()` saves the last plot (or a named plot object) to a file:

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| include: true
#| eval: false
ggsave("plot.png", plot = my_plot, width = 10, height = 8, dpi = 300)
ggsave("plot.pdf", plot = my_plot, width = 10, height = 8)
```

| Argument | Description |
|---|---|
| `filename` | output file path (extension sets format) |
| `plot` | ggplot object (default: last plot) |
| `width`, `height` | dimensions |
| `units` | `"in"` (default), `"cm"`, `"mm"`, `"px"` |
| `dpi` | resolution — raster formats only |
| `device` | `"png"`, `"pdf"`, `"jpeg"`, `"tiff"`, `"svg"` |

> 📝 **Task 9** — export one of your plots; try different formats and dimensions

## Final Exercise: Gene Density along Chromosomes

1. Import gene annotation data from a BED file
2. Filter to chromosomes chr1–chr6
3. Bar plot of gene counts per chromosome
4. Histogram / density plot of gene positions — faceted by chromosome
5. Export to PDF

## Step 1: Import and inspect

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| include: true
#| eval: false
genes <- read.table("data/genes.bed", header = FALSE)
colnames(genes) <- c("chrom", "start", "end", "name", "score", "strand")
head(genes)
```

## Step 2: Filter chromosomes

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| include: true
#| eval: false
genes_filtered <- subset(genes,
  chrom %in% c("chr1", "chr2", "chr3", "chr4", "chr5", "chr6"))
cat("Number of genes in chr1–chr6:", nrow(genes_filtered), "\n")
```

## Step 3: Bar plot of gene counts

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| include: true
#| eval: false
ggplot(genes_filtered, aes(x = chrom, fill = chrom)) +
  geom_bar() +
  ggtitle("Number of Genes per Chromosome (chr1–chr6)") +
  xlab("Chromosome") + ylab("Number of Genes") +
  theme_minimal()
```

## Step 4: Faceted gene density

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| include: true
#| eval: false
ggplot(genes_filtered, aes(x = start)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 50, fill = "steelblue", alpha = 0.8) +
  facet_wrap(~ chrom, scales = "free_x") +
  ggtitle("Gene Start Position Distribution by Chromosome") +
  xlab("Genomic position") + ylab("Gene count") +
  theme_minimal()
```

## Step 5: Export

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| include: true
#| eval: false
p_final <- ggplot(genes_filtered, aes(x = start)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 50, fill = "steelblue", alpha = 0.8) +
  facet_wrap(~ chrom, scales = "free_x") +
  theme_minimal()

ggsave("gene_density.pdf", p_final, width = 12, height = 8)
```

## Resources

- R Graph Gallery: <https://www.r-graph-gallery.com/>
- R Gallery Book: <https://bookdown.org/content/b298e479-b1ab-49fa-b83d-a57c2b034d49/>
- R for Data Science — Visualisation: <https://r4ds.had.co.nz/data-visualisation.html>
